about: Indian Overseas Bank was founded in 1937 by Chidambaram Chettyar. It was one of the 14 major banks that were nationalized in 1969. [1]

key_points.raw:
ABOUT [ edit ]

Indian Overseas Bank was founded in 1937 by Chidambaram Chettyar. It was one of the 14 major banks that were nationalized in 1969. [1]

KEY POINTS [ edit ]

Business Mix Q3 FY26[1]
Advances: 46%
Term Deposits: 32%
Savings Bank: 18%
Current Deposits: 4%

Key Ratios
CRAR: 16.30% in Q3 FY26 vs 13.83% in FY22
NIM: 3.32% in Q3 FY26 vs 2.41% in FY22[2]
Gross NPA: 1.54% in Q3 FY26 vs 9.82% in FY22
Net NPA: 0.24% in Q3 FY26 vs 2.65% in FY22
PCR: 97.49% in Q3 FY26 vs 91.66% in FY22[3] [4]

Improvement in Asset Quality[5][6]
Fresh slippages to NPA category has reduced from Rs 2,756 Cr in Q4 FY25 to Rs 301 Cr in Q3 FY26. The slippage ratio of the Bank reduced by 108 bps to 0.11% in Q3 FY26 vs 1.19% in Q4 FY25.

Branch Network
As of Q3 FY26, the bank operates ~3,438 branches, ~3622 ATMs, and ~11,959 business correspondents across India vs ~3,200 branches, 3,350 ATMs, and 2,650 business correspondents in FY22.
Branch Breakup
Semi-Urban: 31%
Rural: 27%
Metro: 21%
Urban: 21% [7][8]

It has opened 22 specialized SME branches across PAN India, identified 144 MSME branches with growth potential, and designated them as MSME-focused branches. [9]

Customer Base
As of Q1 FY25, the bank has a base of 41 Mn active customers vs 39 Mn active customers in FY22. [10] [11]

Loan Book
As of Q3 FY26, the total advances stand at Rs. ~2,94,974 Cr vs Rs. ~1,55,800 Cr in FY22.
Loan book breakup
Agri: 34% in Q3 FY26 vs 27% in FY22
Retail: 30% in Q3 FY26 vs 26% in FY22
MSME: 18% in Q3 FY26
Corporate/ others: 28% in Q3 FY26[12][13]

Deposits
In Q3 FY26, total deposits stood at Rs. ~3,49,302 Cr vs Rs. 2,62,100 Cr in FY22.
CASA (Global): 40.85% in Q3 FY26 vs 43.4% in FY22 [14][15]

International Business
The bank has 4 Overseas Branches, one each in Singapore, Hong Kong, Bangkok, and Colombo. It also has a 35% JV in Malaysia undergoing voluntary winding up. [16] [17] Overseas advances accounted for ~5.5% to its global advances in Q3 FY26.[12]

Insurance Business
The bank owns an 18% stake in Universal Sompo General Insurance Co. Ltd which is a non-life insurance business. Other investors of Universal Sompo include Indian Bank, Dabur Investment, Sompo Japan, and Karnataka Bank. Universal Sompo has a bancassurance tie-up with Allahabad Bank & Karnataka Bank. [18]

New Products
In FY24, the bank launched new products “Personal Loan Top Up scheme” for its ‘A category’ Personal Loan borrowers with a maximum loan of Rs. 7.50 Lakh, Capital Light Asset Product “Loan Against Sovereign Gold Bond”, etc. [19]

Digital Outreach
The bank has introduced new digital initiatives such as “Tab Banking”, “My Account My Name”, “Instant Safe Deposit Locker Allotment” through a web-based portal, etc. As of Q3 FY26, the bank has 38.50 lakh internet banking registered users and 121.65 lakh mobile banking registered users. [20][21]

GoI Shareholding
The bank was put under Prompt Corrective Action (PCA) in Oct 2015 due to its high NPA. Over FY18-21, GoI infused Rs 22,974 Cr into the bank. This has helped the bank to improve its capital ratios and meet regulatory requirements. In Sept 2021, the bank was taken out of the PCA. As of Jun 2024, the GOI holds ~92.4% stake in the company. [22] [23] [24] [25]

Ministry of Finance made a significant divestment of shares in the Indian Overseas Bank. with Offer for Sale on the 17th and 18th of December 2025. The transaction involved selling over 417 million shares, which effectively lowered the government’s stake from approximately 94.61% to 92.44%.[26]

Tax Notice[27]
Bank has received a Notice of Demand from the Deputy Commissioner of Income-tax, Chennai, for Assessment Year 2015-16, amounting to Rs. 766 Cr. This demand arises from a re-computation of income and disallowances of claims made in the bank's tax returns, following an Income Tax Appellate Tribunal order dated 31 December 2024. The bank is in the process of challenging the order before the appropriate forum, asserting that it possesses adequate legal and factual grounds to substantiating its position. Based on this, the bank expects the demand to subside and anticipates no material impact on its financial or operational activities.
